The PM’s Untapped Strategic Authority; and Why AI Changes Everything

A note on process: The ideas, frameworks, and voice in this piece are my own, drawn from lived experience in project management and organizational leadership. I write with the assistance of AI tools as part of a deliberate and transparent approach to content development, consistent with how I believe modern practitioners will increasingly work. AI serves as a structural and editorial collaborator. Full ownership of the content, perspective, and judgment expressed here remains with me.

Not long ago, I got word that our IT department was proposing to eliminate our project management platform (the tool my team had built our entire workflow around) in favor of a different solution already bundled in our existing software suite. The stated reason: cost savings.
The easy response would have been to shrug, start preparing for the migration, and begin the quiet work of rebuilding everything we’d created in a new tool. That’s what a coordinator does; they absorb the decision and execute. Instead, I started asking questions, and what I found stopped the conversation in its tracks.
A feature-equivalent migration to the proposed replacement didn’t just cost the same, it cost more. Twenty percent more, to be exact. The “savings” narrative only worked if you compared against the entry-level tier of the new tool, which lacked the portfolio management, roadmapping, and dependency tracking capabilities my team actually used. No one had done that math. No one, it turned out, had been asked to.
But I didn’t stop there. I also surfaced a second issue that no one had named yet: our access to the current platform was provisioned through IT’s account, which meant any decision they made about the tool could remove our primary project management infrastructure without requiring our input or consent. That was a governance problem; one that existed regardless of what happened with the proposed migration. The real recommendation wasn’t just “don’t migrate” — it was “our team needs its own independent license so that our operational continuity is never subject to a unilateral decision made by another part of the organization.”
I brought all of it to leadership in a formal issue paper: cost analysis, capability gap matrix, known reliability risks, and a four-part recommendation. That’s what shifted the conversation.
I’m sharing this not to highlight one win, but because it illustrates something I believe deeply about the project management profession: the information and positioning to influence decisions at the highest level is already in your hands. Most PMs just aren’t using it.

The Misunderstanding at the Heart of the PM Role
Here’s what I want executives to hear: your project manager is one of the most informed people in your organization, and most of you don’t know it.
A senior PM sits at the intersection of everything. They know the technical constraints your engineers are navigating. They know the stakeholder tensions your business partners aren’t saying out loud. They’ve seen the early warning signs in three previous projects that looked exactly like this one. They know where the bodies are buried, where the optimism is inflated, and where the real risk lives.
A coordinator tracks tasks. A strategic asset holds the full picture AND knows what to do with it. The PM profession has always been capable of being more than a coordinator; most organizations just haven’t asked for it, and most PMs haven’t pushed for it either.
And here’s what I want PMs to hear: most of you are not operating like the strategic asset you actually are.
The gap between what PMs know and what they actually say in the room is one of the most persistent (and self-inflicted) problems in the profession. The habit of translating “I have a recommendation” into “I’ll flag that as a risk,” and then watching the decision get made without you, is a mindset issue that quietly erodes the level of influence and leadership you actually hold within your projects.

Why PMs Hold Back
Before we talk about the solution, let’s be honest about some of the biggest causes.
PMs don’t hold back because they’re not smart enough. They hold back because:
The role was defined narrowly. For decades, project management methodology trained us to facilitate, track, and report. The PM was positioned as the process keeper rather than the opinion holder, and that framing runs deep, even in people who know better.
The culture punishes the messenger. In many organizations, the PM who pushes back on an executive’s decision is taking a real risk. You can be seen as difficult, not a team player, too negative. The path of least resistance is to document and comply.
Influence without authority is hard. PMs rarely own the budget, the headcount, or the final call. Making a strong recommendation when you don’t have formal power requires a kind of confidence that most people have to deliberately build.
All of that is real, but so is this: None of it changes the fact that silence is a choice AND it has consequences.
When PMs consistently defer to executive judgment without contributing their strategic perspective, projects drift, decisions get made without the full picture, problems that were visible weeks ago surface as crises. And the PM who saw it coming absorbs the blame for an outcome they weren’t empowered to prevent.

The Unique Vantage Point
Let me make the case for why PMs are positioned to do something that no one else in the organization can do quite the same way.
Executives have vision but limited operational visibility. The 20,000 foot view. Engineers have deep technical knowledge but a narrow view of business priorities. Finance has the numbers but not the narrative. Sales has the customer voice but not the delivery reality. They could see the forest, but for the trees.
The PM is the only person whose job requires them to hold all of it at once.
Think of it this way: the executives are at 20,000 feet with their eyes on the end goal. The SMEs are in the trees, heads down, marching through the forest because that’s their day-to-day. The PM is the navigator on the ground; map in hand, walkie-talkie live, translating what’s coming from the top and leading the expedition through the terrain to its final destination.
You are required to understand the technical, the financial, the human, and the strategic; simultaneously. You have to synthesize across functions because no one else is accountable for the whole picture, zooming out to see the big picture from the top, and zooming in to the details to keep that picture in the frame. Far from administrative work, that synthesis is the highest-value cognitive work in the building.
The question is: are you doing anything with that position?

Where AI Enters; and Gives You More Leverage
This is where I want to reframe what most of the “AI for PMs” conversation misses.
Most of the discourse about AI in project management focuses on efficiency: automated status reports, AI-generated meeting summaries, smarter Gantt charts. That’s real, and it matters; reclaiming an hour or two a day of administrative time is genuinely valuable. But that’s light work and really only opens up slightly more bandwidth.
When you use that newfound time to dive deeper, AI removes the last remaining excuse for PMs to walk into a room unprepared.
Think about what used to take days:

• Synthesizing feedback from dozens of stakeholder interviews.

• Pulling trend data across multiple projects to identify patterns.

• Drafting scenario analyses that compare three different approaches with trade-offs clearly laid out.

• Building the kind of evidence-based case that makes an executive sit up and actually engage.
Those tasks weren’t impossible before, they were just expensive; in time, in effort, in cognitive bandwidth. So most PMs skipped them and/or handed that work off to an analyst to deal with (if they had one). They showed up with the deck that had the green/yellow/red status indicators, and they left the strategic framing on the table.
AI changes that cost structure dramatically. The PM who uses these tools well shows up differently; with the synthesis already done, the recommendation already formed, the data to back it, the risks mapped, and the options clearly laid out. That’s someone an executive actually wants in the room, AND it’s the PM you were always capable of being.

This Is What “More Than” Looks Like in Practice
Let me make this concrete with two moments from my own experience; moments where the PM’s strategic position and the right use of AI combined to produce something neither could have delivered alone.
The first is the platform story I opened with. When I walked into that conversation, I didn’t lead with “I disagree with the proposed migration.” I led with “here’s what the math actually shows, here’s the governance risk nobody named, and here are four specific recommendations.” The position was the same either way; what changed was the preparation behind it and the frame I put around it, and that’s what got heard.
What made the preparation possible was AI. Timeliness was the critical factor; leadership needed to make their next move quickly, and I needed to produce a defensible cost analysis and feature comparison fast. AI assisted in the full evaluation of both platforms against our team’s actual workflow and integration requirements, surfacing key insights that went into the issue paper. What would have taken days of manual analysis came together in hours; AI helped give my thinking real speed to deliver the insights faster.
The second is a performance targeting methodology I developed after noticing a structural flaw in how our organization set stretch goals. We were using current-year trends to project next year’s targets, which meant our goals were always anchored to where we’d been rather than where the environment was heading. I proposed anchoring stretch targets to the maximum escalation ceiling the regulatory framework allowed instead; closing the lag and setting a baseline that reflected real conditions rather than trailing performance.
This is where AI really earned its place: the methodology required stress-testing complex math AND a thorough review of several large, publicly available regulatory framework documents to assess utility and defensibility. AI handled both, giving me the analytical confidence to walk into that conversation knowing the recommendation would hold up under scrutiny. Leadership didn’t push back; they adopted the methodology as the new standard.
Neither of those outcomes happened because I had the authority to change anything. They happened because I had done the thinking, understood the system well enough to diagnose what wasn’t working, and was willing to say what I saw out loud, with AI giving me the speed and analytical depth to back it up.
That’s the pattern worth building. When you’re sitting in a room and you have a perspective that the people around you don’t have, the strategic move is to share it clearly and with enough support behind it that it can be evaluated on its merits. You’re not lobbying. You’re contributing the one thing you’re uniquely positioned to contribute: the synthesized, cross-functional, ground-truth view that nobody else in the room has. A sentence worth keeping in your back pocket for exactly that moment: “Before we finalize that, I want to share one thing I’m seeing from where I sit. It’ll be brief.” What comes after it is your expertise.

A Note to Executives
If you’ve read this far and you manage project managers, I want to leave you with something to sit with.
The next time you’re in a project review and your PM is walking you through a status deck, ask yourself: Am I creating conditions where this person can tell me what they actually think?
Because if your PM feels like their role is to report rather than to advise, that’s a design problem, and the cost of t
